slots.text: AI video tools are everywhere now but they're solving the wrong problem. Nobody needs another way to make generic content. People need help making videos that actually matter to their audience.

Things that break when you try to validate an AI video app:

1. Users say they want it but never use demos
2. Everyone compares you to whatever launched yesterday
3. The tech moves faster than user feedback
4. Hard to know if excitement is real or just AI hype

The hardest part about building AI video isn't the AI part. It's figuring out what people actually want to create vs what they think they want. Everyone says they want professional videos but most just need something that doesn't look like garbage.

Interesting pattern: when you show people AI video demos, they immediately start thinking about content they've never made before instead of improving content they already make. Says something about creative constraints.
Most AI video apps feel like solutions in search of problems. Starting with user interviews instead of cool tech demos probably saves months of building the wrong thing.

Early signals that an AI video idea might work:

1. People describe specific use cases without prompting
2. They ask about pricing before you mention it
3. They want to know when they can start using it
4. They share examples of current painful workarounds
AI video tools are everywhere but most solve problems nobody has. The real challenge isn't making AI generate video — it's figuring out what people actually want to create and why.

Testing an AI video app and hitting the classic validation wall: early users say they love it, but usage drops off a cliff after day 3. Either the novelty wears off or we're missing the actual workflow people need.
People ask for AI video features they think they want, then use completely different ones when you actually build them. The gap between stated and revealed preferences is massive in creative tools.

What kills AI video apps:
1. Processing times that break creative flow
2. Output quality that's almost good enough
3. No clear use case beyond "this is cool"
4. Pricing that assumes enterprise budgets
The AI video space feels like mobile apps in 2009. Everyone knows something big is happening, but most attempts are just desktop software crammed into a new format.

Watching people test the video app: they spend 10 minutes exploring features, get excited, then can't think of what to actually make. The tool works fine. The 'why would I use this' question doesn't have a clear answer yet.

The biggest problem with AI video apps isn't the tech anymore. It's that everyone thinks they need Netflix-level production value when most people just want their weird idea to not look like garbage. The bar is way lower than founders think.
Video generation is getting commoditized fast. The real moat is in the editing interface and understanding what people actually want to create.

Things nobody tells you about AI video apps:

1. Rendering times still suck
2. Users expect instant everything
3. Storage costs eat your margins
4. Most generated clips are 3 seconds of mediocrity
Interesting pattern: people describe what they want in video using completely different language than how AI models are trained. There's a translation layer missing that nobody's solving properly.

The feedback loop on video is brutal. You wait 2 minutes for a render, hate it, tweak one thing, wait again. Compare that to text where you see changes instantly. This fundamental UX problem is why most AI video tools feel clunky.
Everyone's building the video creation tool. Almost nobody's thinking about the video management problem that comes after.
The hardest part about building an AI video app isn't the AI. It's figuring out what people actually want to make videos about. Most founders skip this step and wonder why nobody uses their tool.

What I learned this week testing video AI features:

1. People care more about speed than perfection
2. Audio quality matters way more than I thought
3. Export formats break deals faster than bugs do
4. Everyone assumes it should be free

The video generation models are getting scary good, but they're also becoming commoditized fast. If your moat is just 'we use the best model,' you don't have a moat. The real value is in the workflow around the generation.
Noticed something weird: users will wait 3 minutes for a video to generate but abandon after 10 seconds if the preview doesn't load. Patience is contextual.
Building with AI feels like building on quicksand sometimes. The capabilities change so fast that your product assumptions can be wrong within weeks.

Three things that kill AI video apps:

1. Thinking generation quality is the only metric that matters
2. Making the UI as complex as professional video software
3. Not having a clear answer to 'why not just use ChatGPT?'
AI video apps are having their 2010 photo filter moment. Everyone thinks the tech is the product. The tech is table stakes.

Things nobody warns you about with AI video:
1. Render times still suck at scale
2. Users expect Netflix quality from beta models
3. Storage costs eat your margins
4. Every demo breaks in front of investors
The core problem with AI video isn't the AI part. It's that most people don't actually need custom video content. They need better distribution for the videos they already have.
Noticed something weird: users who create the most AI videos share the fewest. The ones who share the most barely use the AI features. There's a signal here.
Building an AI video app feels like opening a restaurant because you found a good tomato supplier. The tomato matters, but can you actually cook?

What actually matters for AI video adoption:
1. Export time under 30 seconds
2. One-click sharing to platforms
3. Templates that don't look like templates
4. Undo that actually works
The AI video space feels like everyone's building the same product with different UI colors. Most founders are copying features instead of solving actual user problems.

Things nobody tells you about AI video apps:
1. Processing costs eat your margins alive
2. Users expect Hollywood quality from day one
3. Storage becomes your biggest headache
4. Rendering times kill user retention

The real challenge isn't making AI generate decent videos. It's making users care enough to wait 3 minutes for processing when TikTok gives instant gratification. Patience is the scarcest resource in 2026.

Noticed something: users who create their first AI video on mobile never switch to desktop. But users who start on desktop often want mobile editing later. Platform choice in the first 60 seconds predicts everything.

What kills AI video startups:
1. Underestimating compute costs
2. Overestimating how much users will pay
3. Building features before finding product-market fit
4. Assuming viral content creates sticky users
Most AI video apps are solutions looking for problems. The winning approach isn't better AI - it's figuring out which specific use case people will actually pay for consistently.
Building an AI video app is teaching me that most people overestimate how much AI they actually want in their tools. They want the output, not the experience of wrestling with prompts.

What nobody tells you about AI video apps:

1. Rendering infrastructure costs eat your margins alive
2. Users expect Hollywood quality from iPhone-quality inputs
3. The AI part is table stakes, distribution is everything
4. Legal questions around training data keep lawyers busy

The cold start problem with AI video is brutal. You need users to train the model, but the model sucks without users. Chicken and egg, except the chicken costs $50k/month in compute and the egg might never hatch.

Interesting pattern: when people describe what they want from AI video tools, they always reference existing content. They want to remake something, not create something new. The creative leap still happens in human brains.
The hardest part of validating an AI video app isn't technical feasibility. It's figuring out if people will pay premium prices for something that feels like magic but costs like enterprise software.

AI video app reality check:

1. Demo videos get 100k views
2. Signups convert at 2%
3. Paying customers convert at 0.1%
4. Math is unforgiving
5. Back to the drawing board
